speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three with Lipman Radzik. You are still in Auschwitz. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Auschwitz itself, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was the most horrendous, the most horrifying things, but those it was. You have to realize there were people there from all over the world, from Jews from all the European countries. And the horror of those people came there, which they couldn't actually [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] face it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was such a terrible thing. Beside they lost their families, they lost their loved ones. And your mind actually didn't know what to do first. Shall I think about my wife and children? Shall I think my parents? Shall I think about my loved ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, was the fight to survive. The sight is-- you saw the horror. Those which couldn't work anymore-- I passed by day in, day out by the crematoriums. They were-- you smelled the flesh of those people, which went direct-- women and children and others. Which-- it wasn't only us to be survive physically for your body. It was, also-- they were playing it havoc with your life. Today you're here. Tomorrow you'll finish up there. Well, the Germans used to laugh sarcastically. Well, you will fly out to Moses through-- through the chimney. I passed by and there was about four meters high of flame was coming out from those crematorium. I passed by there every day. [INAUDIBLE] well, most probably next day you will be there. And you'll see all your friends, which they couldn't work anymore and there were those selections. Those which died doing the work, and those which they finally-- there. In the camp itself, there was at night, that he went out-- doctors, he used to come-- Mengele and some others-- used to come and they would select out the [INAUDIBLE] only was just a bag of bones. How long can your body hold out that kind of work, that kind of terrible mental--

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, it was in three parts. It was physically, mentally, and spiritually. All those three parts. There were three fronts. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which one to fight first, which played actually havoc with you? And the selections, you know-- next day, oh my God, my friend is gone. Oh my God, he died [INAUDIBLE] in the middle of the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my brother, [CRYING] it's my youngest brother, and my mother begged me and said, take good care of him. He was such a wonderful hu-- human being. The youngest, his name was Beresh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was in a different working commando, and he was in a different one. It was winter. And they gave you to wear those wooden shoes, which were made in Holland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was [INAUDIBLE]. It was-- was horrifying thing. This killed thousands of people without socks. And your feet got so much-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by rubbing at your foot against that-- that wood was horrible. Your feet will swell up and you will get-- horrible. You couldn't walk anymore. And to work on it. And this-- it was also part of the-- the horrifying pain that they want to instill in you. Those-- And one day, my brother had come to see me, when he was in the next block to me. My God, I didn't recognize him. And I saw it. It was winter. And his foot-- one foot was so swell up. He said to me, 'I cannot go anymore.' It was just-- puss was coming out. His foot was so swell up, twice the size. And I begged him, 'Don't switch your mind in the camp.' In the barracks, the following day, they just took you out and automatically you went to the crematorium. There was, every day, [INAUDIBLE] couldn't work. And I begged him he should go work. [CRYING] He couldn't go to work. [SOBBING] And I came at night. He wasn't there anymore. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Here, I had friends I'd-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was actually the last link of my family. And I holded-- I holded onto him so. [SOBBING] Half the time I used to take from my little piece of bread, and I will try to share it with him. It was terrible. He was young. He was much younger than I am. He must have been about 14 years, maybe. 14, 15 years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Such a beautiful-- he used to encourage me. And he stayed in, and that was the end of him. I didn't see him anymore. Actually, I had some friends where I was want to commit suicide on myself. And I had there a-- a good friend of mine. I don't know. I think-- the only thing what I remember he was from Czechoslovakia. He went from Pressburg. He was a professor. And he was next to me. We were next to the-- where we slept, or next to me. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he saw that I was losing it my mind. And I'm going down and down. And he had a terrible inspiration on me. He said to me, 'That's what the Germans want. All of the families here are gone. You have to fight him to survive for your family alone.' And he-- day in and day out-- he had tremendous impact on me. And physically, it was impossible to survive. Was-- was mentally. That he-- every day, he said, 'The war will come to an end. You have to hold on to it, and hold on to it.' And finally, he got me into such a point that I was, well, if I shall die, I will die at least a free man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And actually, I called myself after the war-- when I spoke in many-- I called myself the one which got away. I said, Hitler didn't want that I should be standing here, in front of you in this auditorium, with so many people of you, for the armed forces, that I should stand here and expose the horrifying suffering, the horrendous thing with the humiliation. What this-- those people have actually put on-- on those victimized people. He didn't want me I should tell you. And I-- somehow I [INAUDIBLE] I said, I call myself, the one which got away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get away? How did you decide not to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, after-- I had a number of friends. And I-- from the beginning, I was much stronger. I used to get at night-- even in dark, we will sit together. I would read for him poetry, and I would tell him things of human behavior, that you can lift yourself up above your physical needs. Because, actually, what keeps you going is your mind. Don't let your mind die on you. If you let your mind die-- because your mind is the-- the main-- what do you call it-- the which directs your life, from walking, to speaking, to do anythings. Once your mind loses-- you lose it, your body is finished. Even at this, a bag of bones, but your mind keep on saying it to you, you can do it. I used to encourage a lot. We used to have a group-- it was me and this professor. He was much older than me. And some others that we took over groups to encourage [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] under the slogan, 'Don't give up. They want you, you should give up. They want you to give up right now.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is, somehow, some way, because when I was liberated, I weighed only 70 pound.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Auschwitz, I went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was it the Death March when the Russian was trying already to close in, and they didn't want to find some survivors in Auschwitz. And we went, maybe for two, three weeks. There was thousands of them died on the way. It was winter time. It was in January.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '44. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No, '44? It was in '45. It was in the beginning of '45, correct. And that was till May 8, we were traveling to different kind of camps. Thousands of them died on the way. And that was the most horrid. Those which couldn't walk anymore, the Germans killed them right away. With special squads, where they were going down with their guns, and they shot them right away, near on the-- on the side ways of the road. Thousand-- it was littered with thousands of corpses.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to-- my mind is so much opening chapters of horrors. But every chapter in me say, unravel me. Open me first. Tell my story. So I have a fight with myself, which case should I explain? Which shall I tell, expose? Each one [INAUDIBLE], I'm more important. Tell about me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It so happened to me that I have to mention to you one particular that happened in the-- in the ghetto of Mlawa, that I watched a horrifying thing. The Germans used to come in in the ghetto [INAUDIBLE] of course for collecting all kind of things, whatever it is-- ransom for this, ransom for that, [INAUDIBLE] came in. And once [INAUDIBLE] I was working there in the street there, and a woman was coming out was holding with a child. The child must have been maybe a year or something like this. [INAUDIBLE] You talk about children-- the children in the ghetto. They should have been-- the nourishment for the child was so degraded it couldn't grow. You could see a child of six, seven years, it would look like four, because this is the time when the body develops. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the German walked over to that woman and stayed there with two others, and I saw the most unbelievable thing. And I said to myself, there must be some human being in them. He opened the thing, and there was a chocolate. He unwrapped the chocolate, and gave the child that chocolate. [INAUDIBLE] I was wondering if something is an angel. So he gave the piece of chocolate, and he give it to the child, which is licking that piece of chocolate and feeling like this. Suddenly, I noticed he had a pair of gloves. And a pair of gloves was in his belt, like over folded, like-- I remember like today. He took out the gloves, put on the gloves on his hand. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Suddenly, he has chain-- changed from a human being to a deadly, vicious animal, in one second. He grabbed that poor little boy by a foot. He was still holding that chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were-- in that place was a pump where people used to go for water-- in the middle, a big pump there. And he grabbed the foot with that child, and he knocked it against the whole body. He was swinging around like you see that sport which you take the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the gavel? The things [INAUDIBLE] this, you know, which has a thing like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was twisting it around a number of times, and he hit that little child against that pump, and-- and smashed his head to pieces. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You have a hard time to adjust your mind to switch so quickly from a human being to a vicious animal. Actually an animal wouldn't do that. And he was hysterically laughing what was two other things there. And then he took the thing, and he throwed it away, the child there and then he kicked it over there and was want to make sure. The-- the-- and I saw the mother. She was grabbing her hair, tearing-- [SOBBING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tearing her hair-- hair out, from this, like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took out the gun, and he shot her right in the face. I remember, her face split to pieces. Then, in a most calm way, he took off his gloves, and he throwed it on that child, [INAUDIBLE] like, well, those gloves were contaminated. And he laughed, and the other one took pictures of it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, how can you tell something like this? I must say, there is not a pen, there is not a human mind can actually describe that horrifying picture, which have took place maybe in five minutes' time. All this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it just unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I went home after work, and I got so hysterically in home that my mother didn't think-- didn't know what to do, what happened. She-- I couldn't tell her what I have seen it. There was a-- for a few days, I was in a trance. I couldn't get my senses together what I have seen it. Well, I just have told you, I think it was three or four episodes what I said. And I could tell you many, many horrifying more. And I have to admit to you, my beautiful people, shall your mind say to yourself, that you say to me, this is impossible. It can't be. It's a horrifying dream, I would suggest-- I would have to admit you're right. You're 100% right. If I wouldn't be there, and it wouldn't get ingrained in my mind-- in my thinking-- what I have seen, and I would be on you side, listening, most probably I would have said it is the unbelievable thing. It is the unbelievable which the human mind cannot absorb. It's not able to tell, to relate somehow, somewhere. I, myself, even now, after so many years, I have a fight in my mind. For one side, the horror part, which is ingrained in my mind. I wish I could press a button [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and to be able to erase-- to erase that terrible cha-- [CRYING] terrible, terrible chapter. And I feel like that I'm-- my mind is not equipped. It's not created to be able to tell, and to be the spokesman for all those horrifying things what I have seen-- not only in personally myself, for all the six million which they're suffering. I only apologize to them [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I tried to-- to represent them and their horror, and tell to the world [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what I personally-- what all of them went through, and those horrifying years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from the Death March?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Death March? The Death March was-- and I'm glad you asked me this. I had a double fight with me. I have seen it that the end is close. We could hear, you know, artillery and the airplanes. We saw that the end is coming. Was-- there were-- the Germans, himself, have seen. They start to get panicky, but the day of reckoning will come. And I said to myself that I am losing it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My own preservation to be able to make it to the last, because the Germans said, shall we lose the war, five minutes before 12, you will also perish. But that was actually their main purpose. And I was feeling it that mine end is also coming because I saw there is so many left and right, and horrifying things, thousands in felling it on the sides of the roads. And I said, 'I'm not going to make it. I feel I'm not going to make it.' [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Somehow, somewhere, my blessed mother's face, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my grandfather, and some of the others-- I saw my mother's face, kept on saying, 'You have to make it. You have to make it.' And I was bitterly crying. I said, 'I can't anymore.' Many time I fell and I picked myself up and I kept on going. I-- this, the final chapter of the evacuation, or we call it the Death March from Auschwitz, was, like someone said, that was nearly the straw what broke the camel's back. You couldn't take it anymore. But somehow, somewhere, they were in me that my parents and that good friend in that-- in Auschwitz-- the one which I had, the-- the professor. He was a professor in university in-- in Pressburg-- had instilled in me physically impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: When doctors came out after and said to me, 'How did you survive?' I had no answer, actually, as to how I survived. Maybe the last drop milk for my dear mother which kept me going, and the kind words. But one thing I had it in my mind that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I will have to tell the world at least what transpired, what horrendous thing happened to humanity, what happened to our people. Then I can die. Where did you go on this Death March?

speaker: interviewee
text: We actually went through-- from Poland, because they all took us into the Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were many cities and many thing. I actually don't remember many names for many of them. Because they were hoping that the-- that the Allies will not get into Germany. And they didn't want that we should be left as living witnesses to tell to the world [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what transpired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did liberation find you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The liberation-- actually when I was liberated, I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was in a trance. When finally the Germans left us and they said, you're free-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] this is another horrifying chapter, which I would like to dwell on it a little bit and tell it to the world what actually happened after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we-- we talk about that, where were you when you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated at Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: That day of liberation, I remember we were in a certain barracks there. And we supposed to be actually killed. And they had put an explosive around our barracks to destroy us. But it so happened that the Russians came in. It was a day or two before, after I was told. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was, also, a miracle itself that we were saved. Well, the day of the liberation was a sort-- it wasn't of jubilation. You were-- you didn't know what to do with yourself. There were nowhere to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, finally I was told I still even walked, often, in this Theresienstadt that I was still hoping the Germans does not walk behind me, follow me. The shadow of the horror of death followed you around from all the sides. It took a long time to be able until, well, they start to give us bread. And, of course, a lot-- thousands of them died from the food which we couldn't actually-- our bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian Armed Forces. And there were many Russian from our faith, Jewish officers. They were bitterly crying. They couldn't believe it. You have seen it. You have seen it. Few thousands of us were there were actually living skeletons-- living skeletons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They cried with us, and they tried to help as much as they could. Of course, thousands of them died after the war because their bodies couldn't take the food of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after liberation, there is another horror, a chapter which I feel like that the free world have failed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds like this is probably going to be--
